text:
HEMATOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

PATIENT: Margaret Okonkwo
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
DIVISION: Genitourinary Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine oncology follow-up for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma. Patient presents for reassessment approximately 3 months after initiating cabozantinib (Cabometyx) as second-line therapy. Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis results reviewed at today's visit.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman with a history of stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), right kidney, with sarcomatoid differentiation comprising approximately 20% of the tumor burden, presenting today for routine restaging and toxicity assessment on cabozantinib 40 mg orally daily.

Brief oncologic history: She was diagnosed approximately 12 months ago with a large right renal mass (11.4 cm on staging CT) with involvement of the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with hematogenous metastases, establishing stage IV (pT3b pN2 M1) disease at presentation. She underwent open right radical nephrectomy with regional lymphadenectomy with cytoreductive intent, and final surgical pathology confirmed ISUP/WHO grade 4 ccRCC, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, extensive coagulative necrosis (>50%), and 3 of 5 lymph nodes positive. Tumor genomic profiling by comprehensive paired tumor-normal NGS revealed VHL loss-of-function mutation, BAP1 frameshift deletion (with loss of BAP1 protein expression by IHC), PBRM1 splice-site loss-of-function mutation, SETD2 p.R1625H, KDM5C truncating mutation, TP53 p.R248W (subclonal, enriched in sarcomatoid component), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and high-level MET amplification (copy number 7.2). TMB 6.4 mut/Mb; MSS; PD-L1 CPS 14.

Given intermediate IMDC risk classification, sarcomatoid histology, and favorable immunologic features (BAP1 loss, PD-L1 positivity), she was initiated on first-line ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg + nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance, per CheckMate-214 protocol. Treatment was complicated by grade 2 immune-related dermatitis (cycle 3, managed with topical triamcinolone without systemic steroids), and grade 2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism (cycle 4, asymptomatic, initiated on levothyroxine, dose subsequently titrated to 75 mcg daily). After 4 induction cycles she demonstrated a partial response in her pulmonary metastases. Unfortunately, approximately 9 months into therapy, repeat restaging CT demonstrated radiographic disease progression with significant enlargement of pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe dominant lesion from 0.9 cm to 2.4 cm), new bilateral pulmonary nodules, and progression of right pelvic sidewall and new right external iliac lymphadenopathy. An MRI brain obtained at the time of progression showed no intracranial metastases. She was symptomatic at that time with increased dyspnea on exertion and new dry cough.

Given progression through checkpoint blockade, combined with the finding of high-level MET amplification on tumor NGS, she was transitioned to cabozantinib (Cabometyx), a multikinase inhibitor with potent activity against VEGFR2, MET, and AXL, at 60 mg orally daily. Over the first several weeks of therapy, she experienced grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (bilateral palmar and plantar surfaces with blistering and difficulty ambulating), grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools per day above baseline), and grade 2 hypertension (BPs 152-162/94-100 mmHg). The hand-foot syndrome and diarrhea prompted dose reduction to 40 mg orally daily, which was well-tolerated. Hypertension was managed by increasing amlodipine from 5 mg to 10 mg daily with good subsequent blood pressure control. The podiatry service was also consulted.

She returns today for her 3-month restaging visit. Overall, she feels notably better compared to the time of progression. She specifically endorses significant improvement in her dyspnea; she can now walk 2 to 3 blocks on a flat surface without stopping, compared to limiting breathlessness with less than one block of ambulation at the time cabozantinib was initiated. Her dry cough has also improved markedly. She denies hemoptysis, chest pain, fever, chills, or new bone pain. She reports persistent but mild discomfort in the soles of her feet, predominantly at the heels, which she manages with thick-soled gel insoles and application of 40% urea cream (Keralac) twice daily. She reports on average 2 to 3 looser stools per day above her baseline, which is controllable and not significantly disruptive. She does not require loperamide on a daily basis at this point. She denies any mucositis, nausea, or vomiting.

Her fatigue is present but manageable -- she describes it as a moderate afternoon fatigue, typically a 4-5 out of 10 in severity. She is able to perform most activities of daily living independently. She notes mild bilateral wrist and finger achiness, a residual complaint from her time on nivolumab maintenance that has slightly persisted; she manages with acetaminophen as needed (no NSAIDs given CKD). Her blood sugars at home have been in the 110-130 mg/dL fasting range. She is checking her blood pressure at home with a cuff; most readings in the 128-138/78-86 mmHg range on amlodipine 10 mg.

Medications: cabozantinib 40 mg oral daily, levothyroxine 75 mcg oral daily (taken on empty stomach), amlodipine 10 mg oral daily, metformin 500 mg oral twice daily, ferrous sulfate (deferred pending labs today -- was not yet initiated), acetaminophen 500-1000 mg oral as needed.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue as noted above, grade 1, afternoon predominant. No fever, chills, or unintentional weight loss. Weight stable.
Respiratory: Improved dyspnea on exertion, walks 2-3 blocks. Residual mild dry cough, significantly improved. No hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema. Blood pressure controlled at home.
Gastrointestinal: 2-3 loose stools per day above baseline, no frank diarrhea requiring loperamide most days. No nausea, vomiting, mucositis, or blood in stool. Appetite adequate.
Musculoskeletal: Mild bilateral palmar tenderness and heel tenderness from hand-foot syndrome, grade 1 currently. Mild bilateral wrist and finger aches, grade 1.
Skin: Erythema and peeling at bilateral heels, controlled with urea cream. No new rashes, alopecia, or mucosal lesions. No rash resembling the prior truncal eruption from ipilimumab/nivolumab.
Endocrine: No symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism. Compliance with levothyroxine confirmed.
Neurological: No headache, visual changes, new focal neurologic deficits, or seizures.
Genitourinary: No dysuria. Voiding well from the single left kidney.
Psychosocial: Mood reportedly stable. Continuing to see the oncology social worker monthly. Strong family support network.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals: BP 132/80 mmHg, HR 74 bpm (regular), RR 16, SpO2 96% on room air, Temp 36.8°C, Weight 78.2 kg (stable from prior visit), BMI 28.2.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears comfortable.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. No oral mucositis or ulcerations. Oropharynx clear.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated. Thyroid non-tender, non-enlarged.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 normal. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. No lower extremity edema.

Respiratory: Decreased breath sounds at the right base compared to the left, with dullness to percussion at the right lower zone -- consistent with prior history of right nephrectomy site changes and possible mild atelectasis versus pleural changes in the context of prior large resection; however, notably improved compared to the time of progression when right-sided pulmonary metastases were enlarging. No crackles or wheezes. Left lung fields clear.

Abdomen: Well-healed right flank incision from prior radical nephrectomy. Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No abdominal masses appreciated. Bowel sounds present.

Musculoskeletal: Hands -- mild palmar erythema bilaterally, more pronounced over the thenar eminences; no fissuring or frank blistering at this time. Feet -- mild erythema and hyperkeratosis over the bilateral heel pads and metatarsal heads; thickened skin with areas of peeling but no open blistering. Overall consistent with grade 1 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia.

Skin: No truncal or extremity rash. No jaundice.

Neurological: Alert, conversant, cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Gait steady. No focal deficits.

Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy on exam. (Note: prior retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal involvement not assessable on clinical examination.)

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x10^9/L (reference 4.5-11.0)
- Hemoglobin: 10.6 g/dL (low; prior 11.2 g/dL at ~2 months ago -- slight decrease)
- Hematocrit: 32.1%
- MCV: 81 fL (low-normal, borderline microcytic)
- Platelets: 178 x10^9/L
- Neutrophils: 3.4 x10^9/L
- Lymphocytes: 1.6 x10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 22 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.60 mg/dL (eGFR ~45 mL/min/1.73m2, CKD-EPI; stable from last visit)
- BUN: 28 mg/dL
- Glucose: 118 mg/dL (fasting)
- Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL
- Total Protein: 6.8 g/dL
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL
- Total Bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- AST: 38 U/L (ULN 40; borderline -- stable from last check)
- ALT: 42 U/L (ULN 40; grade 1 elevation -- unchanged from prior)
- Alkaline Phosphatase: 72 U/L (normal)
- eGFR comment: Creatinine and eGFR stable from prior visit 4 weeks ago (Cr 1.58). No acute change.

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 2.3 mIU/L (normal; range 0.5-4.5)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

HbA1c: 7.3% (stable; good glycemic control)

Urine protein: Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio sent; result pending (ordered given CKD, TKI-related proteinuria screening).

Iron studies (from today, given ongoing mild anemia):
- Serum iron: 48 mcg/dL (low)
- TIBC: 392 mcg/dL (elevated)
- Transferrin saturation: 12% (low)
- Ferritin: 18 ng/mL (low-normal, borderline)
- Comment: Pattern consistent with iron deficiency anemia superimposed on anemia of CKD/chronic disease. Will initiate iron supplementation.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (reviewed today, obtained approximately 3 months after cabozantinib initiation):

Pulmonary disease: The dominant right lower lobe metastatic nodule has decreased from 2.4 cm at the time of cabozantinib initiation to 1.9 cm -- a meaningful reduction. The right upper lobe nodule has similarly decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.9 cm. The left lower lobe nodule has decreased from 1.5 cm to 1.2 cm. The previously described bilateral small pulmonary nodules are stable or slightly smaller in aggregate. No new pulmonary nodules identified.

Nodal disease: The right pelvic sidewall lymph node has decreased from 1.9 cm to 1.4 cm. The right external iliac lymph node has decreased from 1.4 cm to 1.1 cm. No new pelvic or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy identified.

Abdomen/post-surgical bed: The right nephrectomy bed is unremarkable. No residual retroperitoneal adenopathy above the surgical field. The left kidney appears normal in size, with no masses or hydronephrosis -- important surveillance given prior right nephrectomy and CKD. No hepatic lesions. No adrenal lesions. No ascites.

Bones: No new osseous lesions. Incidental stable degenerative changes at the lumbar spine.

Overall impression: Comparing to the pre-cabozantinib scan, there has been a meaningful decrease in the overall pulmonary and nodal disease burden -- the dominant right lower lobe nodule alone has shrunk by roughly 5 mm, with similar proportional reduction in the other pulmonary and pelvic nodal lesions. This represents a meaningful partial response since starting cabozantinib. No new sites of metastatic disease. Disease is well-controlled at this juncture.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, pT3b pN2 M1, with sarcomatoid differentiation (20%), BAP1 loss, MET amplification -- RESPONDING TO SECOND-LINE CABOZANTINIB

Ms. Okonkwo has had a gratifying response to cabozantinib over the past 3 months. Her imaging today shows a meaningful and clinically significant decrease in disease burden across all assessable lesions -- both pulmonary and nodal -- without any new sites of involvement. This is particularly encouraging given that she progressed through ipilimumab plus nivolumab after an initial partial response, a pattern sometimes seen in patients with aggressive histologic features like sarcomatoid differentiation and TP53 co-mutation (even subclonal). The high-level MET amplification on her tumor NGS profile provides a plausible biologic basis for her response to cabozantinib, which has potent MET inhibitory activity in addition to anti-VEGFR2 and anti-AXL effects.

- Continue cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg orally daily. No dose re-escalation at this time given she has achieved good disease control at 40 mg with manageable toxicity; dose re-escalation to 60 mg would risk exacerbating hand-foot syndrome and diarrhea without clear added benefit, especially in the context of her CKD.
- Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months.
- Continue annual MRI brain surveillance (last MRI brain negative at time of IO progression). No dedicated brain imaging today given no neurologic symptoms.
- No clinical trial match currently available at this institution; will continue to monitor the landscape, particularly for MET-directed trials or next-generation RCC therapeutics.

2. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (PALMAR-PLANTAR ERYTHRODYSESTHESIA), GRADE 1 ON REDUCED-DOSE CABOZANTINIB

Well-controlled at current dose with topical urea-based cream and protective footwear. Patient educated to contact clinic if blistering, ulceration, or severe pain recurs.
- Continue Keralac (40% urea) cream twice daily to bilateral palms and soles.
- Continue gel insoles; avoid narrow-toed footwear.
- Podiatry follow-up as scheduled.
- No dose modification indicated at this time.

3. DIARRHEA, GRADE 1 (TKI-RELATED) -- MANAGEABLE

Significantly improved from grade 2 at initiation. Patient managing with dietary measures and as-needed loperamide.
- Continue as-needed loperamide 2 mg after each loose stool (maximum 16 mg/day) as needed.
- Continue low-residue, low-fat dietary habits as tolerated.
- Adequate oral hydration reinforced.
- No dose modification indicated.

4. HYPERTENSION -- CONTROLLED ON AMLODIPINE 10 MG DAILY

Blood pressure well-controlled at today's visit (132/80) and per home monitoring logs.
- Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily.
- Patient instructed to monitor blood pressure at home and report any consistently elevated readings above 150/90 mmHg.
- If further escalation is needed, consider adding lisinopril (dose-adjusted for CKD) or hydralazine. Note: ACE inhibitors/ARBs may carry additional renoprotective benefit given CKD, but defer given stable BP for now and risk of hyperkalemia.

5. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE, STAGE 3A (eGFR ~45) -- STABLE ON CABOZANTINIB

Creatinine 1.60 mg/dL with eGFR 45, essentially unchanged from the prior visit. This represents a moderate reduction from her presurgical baseline eGFR of 74 (reflecting the combined effect of right nephrectomy and subsequent TKI-related nephrotoxicity). No acute change.
- Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio sent today; result to be reviewed. If significant proteinuria is identified (UPCR > 1.0), nephrology input and/or dose reduction discussion will be entertained.
- Avoid nephrotoxic agents (NSAIDs, IV contrast when feasible, aminoglycosides).
- Metformin at 500 mg twice daily is acceptable for eGFR 45 per current ADA guidelines. No dose change.
- Nephrology co-management to be continued; defer to nephrology for CKD management recommendations.

6. ANEMIA -- IRON DEFICIENCY SUPERIMPOSED ON ANEMIA OF CHRONIC DISEASE

Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, mildly below her prior value of 11.2 g/dL. Iron studies confirm iron deficiency (transferrin saturation 12%, ferritin 18, TIBC elevated). This likely reflects combined TKI-related erythropoiesis suppression, CKD-related anemia, and iron deficiency (potentially dietary or occult GI losses from diarrhea).
- Initiate ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally every other day (alternate-day dosing preferred in CKD to optimize absorption and reduce GI intolerance).
- Stool guaiac: not formally obtained today but will check at next visit given diarrhea history.
- Recheck CBC and iron studies in 6-8 weeks.
- If hemoglobin declines below 9.0 g/dL or patient becomes symptomatic, will consider IV iron infusion (preferable in CKD given poor PO absorption) and/or nephrology/hematology input regarding ESA use.

7. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM -- STABLE ON LEVOTHYROXINE

TSH 2.3 mIU/L, free T4 normal. Well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
- Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg once daily on empty stomach.
- Recheck thyroid function in 3 months at next oncology visit.

8. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS -- WELL-CONTROLLED

HbA1c 7.3%, stable. Fasting glucose 118 mg/dL. No hypoglycemic episodes reported.
- Continue metformin 500 mg twice daily (appropriate for eGFR 45).
- Continue home glucose monitoring.
- Follow-up with primary care provider for annual diabetes-related surveillance.

9. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT

Patient engaged with oncology social worker. She reports good quality of life and strong family support. No acute psychosocial crisis.
- Continue monthly social work meetings.
- No hospice or palliative care referral at this time; patient remains on active treatment with good disease control. Palliative care referral to be considered proactively at any evidence of clinical or psychosocial deterioration.

---

RETURN VISIT

Return to clinic in approximately 4 weeks for interim labs (CBC, CMP, iron studies recheck, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio result review). Next full restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months. Patient provided with oncology nurse triage line for acute toxicity concerns, blood pressure readings consistently above 150/90 mmHg, or any new neurologic symptoms.

---

Time spent: approximately 45 minutes, more than 50% of which was spent in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed by: [Attending Oncologist], MD
Genitourinary Medical Oncology
